lede: Liturgy & Chanting · บทสวด accounts for 97 catalogued manuscripts. It clusters in Phrae (22% of the corpus for this genre), ahead of Lampang and Nan. Nearly all (94%) are written in Tham Lanna · อักษรธรรมล้านนา script. By support it leans to palm-leaf · ใบลาน (69%) over mulberry paper (saa) · กระดาษสา (29%). Dated witnesses run 1541–1964 CE (56 of 97 carry a date).

authored.title: Liturgy & Chanting
authored.body_html:
What this is

This is the performed end of the Buddhist corpus — not scripture to be studied but words to be said aloud. The catalogue files all 97 witnesses as Chanting, and the titles lay out the northern liturgical repertoire.

At its centre is the suat mon (sut mon), the chanted suttas and paritta: the much-loved Mahāsamaya Sutta (Sap mahasamainya), the namo/buddhaguṇa invocations (Tika namo, Putthakun luang), the fifteen-day cycles of recitation. Around it runs the distinctively northern thon — occasional recitations for the crises of life, and above all for death: thon for the newly dead (thon tai, thon phi tai), thon for the house and home (thon ban, thon huean), thon to dispel the inauspicious (sut thon sappakhuet). And at the edges sit the su khwan soul-callings (su khwan for a person, even for an elephant) and the ariradhana invitations to preach — the Kam alathana tham wetsantala, the summons to recite the Vessantara at the Thet Mahachat.

The saiyasat hinge — why this genre matters to the wichaa

This is the genre where canonical Buddhism and efficacious saiyasat are the same performance seen from two sides. A sut thon sappakhuet — a recitation to drive off the inauspicious — a rite against kalakini (bad-luck), a volume that binds sut mon directly to holasat (astrology): these are protective chanting as efficacious action. The companion article on Magic & Ritual describes the benedictory, consecratory end of saiyasat; this genre is that end, filed under "Chanting" because the technology is a spoken formula and the setting is the temple. For this archive's esoteric priorities, the liturgy is not a backwater — it is where the protective and apotropaic core lives in its most public, communal form.

The shape of the collection

Written overwhelmingly in Tham Lanna (94%), with a notable 15% monolingual Pali — the chants themselves are Pali, so a share of the corpus needs no vernacular at all. Its support leans more to paper (29%) than the scholastic genres do, exactly as one would expect of a text opened and read during performance rather than shelved. It is spread evenly across the upper north (Phrae, Lampang and Nan almost level) and reaches back further than most — a witness dated to 1541, the 16th century.

Notes

  • Read it beside saiyasat, and check for hybrids. The thon sappakhuet, kalakini and sut-mon-with-holasat volumes straddle the liturgy / magic boundary; when a chanting title also names a protective or calendrical element, check the raw metadata — the object is often a household ritual manual, not pure liturgy.
  • Heavily funerary and occasional. The thon for the dead, the house and misfortune are the practical working half of this genre — the liturgy of the life-cycle. Splitting the paritta / sut from the thon from the su khwan soul-callings is the obvious next pass, and would separate three quite different performance settings.
  • Linked to the birth-stories by performance. The invitation to preach the Vessantara ties this genre to jātaka at the Thet Mahachat — the same text is a birth-story on one shelf and a recitation cue on another.
